text: We present a case of a laparoscopic resection of the gastroesophageal junction in a patient with recurrent high-grade dysplasia of the distal esophagus and gastroesophageal reflux disease that has been managed for twelve years duration with propan-PAR inhibitors. Radiofrequency ablations have failed. There was recurrent in situ adenocarcinoma. We discussed with the patient the possibility of laparoscopy, laparotomy, thoracotomy. The patient had severe pulmonary disease with chronic obstructive changes and a thoracic procedure was discouraged.

The surgeon begins by freeing the right and left crura of the diaphragm and dissecting the esophagus. The intra-abdominal and distal intrathoracic esophagus is mobilized, taking care to avoid complete devascularization of the inter-thoracic esophagus and proximal stomach.

The surgeon opens the diaphragmatic crux widely to facilitate laparoscopic instrumentation for the esophageal jejunostomy. The distal esophagus and proximal stomach are divided using linear staplers, preserving the stomach's distal, proximal, and mid portions. Endoscopic verification confirms the positioning of the dysplasia area before transecting the stomach in the distal thoracic portion.

The surgeon constructs a Roux limb, bringing it up to the abdomen in an anticholic fashion. An enterotomy and esophagotomy are performed, followed by a side-to-side esophago-jejunostomy using a blue cartridge stapler. The esophago-jejunostomy site is enclosed with a running double layer of varical sutures, aiming to prevent acid reflux and recurrence of dysplasia.

The surgeon checks for leakage using arometinol blue dye. The esophago-jejunostomy is secured to the left crux of the diaphragm. A side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy is performed between the alimentary and biliopancreatic limbs using a linear stapler with white cartridges. Clips are placed to mark the anastomosis for x-ray identification. Drains are positioned in the cytopatic space, and the specimen is bagged to prevent tumor implantation.
